current_fact.fact_text: On April 2, 1865, the Third Battle of Petersburg shattered Confederate lines, forcing the Army of Northern Virginia and the Confederate government to abandon Richmond. Overnight, the Confederate capital went from symbol of defiance to a city of fires and fleeing officials, foreshadowing the Civil War’s end.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

current_fact.match_reason: The collapse of a capital and a long war pairs with an introspective episode about endings, vulnerability, and slow recovery after a shattering turning point.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 1954, a tragic moment at Hermosa Beach, California, was captured when photographer John L. Gaunt documented parents staring out at ocean waves that had swept away their 19‑month‑old child. His stark image, titled "Tragedy by the Sea," won a Pulitzer Prize and became a haunting symbol of human grief against indifferent water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A single, devastating seashore image about the sea’s power connects with a water‑themed episode about immersion and depth, offering a gentler, reflective counterpoint to the ocean’s darker side.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 1956, CBS premiered "As the World Turns" and "The Edge of Night," the first daytime soaps to stretch into a 30‑minute format. The extra minutes turned background melodrama into slow‑burn storytelling, inviting viewers to weave fictional lives into their daily routines for decades.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The birth of longer‑form everyday storytelling on TV echoes Sedna’s own first longform, life‑soundtrack episode where an ongoing narrative of friendship and music begins.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 1964, the Soviet Union launched Zond 1, an unmanned spacecraft aimed at Venus. Although radio contact failed before it could transmit close‑range data, the mission was part of a quiet interplanetary rehearsal, testing how far human engineering could fling a machine into the inner solar system.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

queue.match_reason: A probe flying into the emptiness between worlds lines up with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, both using "space" as a frontier of possibility.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 1972, Charlie Chaplin returned to the United States for the first time in about twenty years, after being labeled a communist and effectively exiled during the Red Scare. The silent film icon arrived as an elderly man, carrying a lifetime of laughter and suspicion in his suitcase.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: Chaplin’s fraught return to the country that once embraced him mirrors an episode about transitions, identity, and belonging under another set of grey, story‑filled skies.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 1992, New York mob boss John Gotti, the immaculately dressed "Teflon Don," was finally convicted of murder and racketeering. The verdict pierced the myth of his invincibility and signaled that even the most cinematic crime empires can end in fluorescent‑lit courtrooms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: The fall of a glamorized criminal underworld reflects a shift in power and space; this parallels an episode about reclaiming nightlife spaces and narratives, especially in hip‑hop, from another angle.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 2, 1992, Serb paramilitary groups massacred at least 42 civilians in the Bosnian town of Bijeljina, an early and chilling signal of the ethnic violence that would tear through Bosnia and Herzegovina. Photographs from the streets captured ordinary houses turned into scenes of terror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The dark, uneasy mood of early 1990s conflict sits uncomfortably close to the brooding, cinematic atmosphere of trip‑hop, a genre that often soundtracked newsreels and late‑night reflections from that era.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 2002, during an intense phase of the Israeli–Palestinian conflict, Israeli forces surrounded the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem after armed Palestinians took refuge inside. The siege turned one of Christianity’s holiest sites into a surreal mix of sanctuary, standoff, and global media stage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A tense clash around a sacred site in the Eastern Mediterranean pairs with flamenco’s tradition of spiritual lament, resistance, and raw emotional storytelling rooted in another crossroads of faith and struggle.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 2004, terrorists linked to the Madrid train bombings attempted another attack, this time targeting Spain’s AVE high‑speed rail line near Madrid. The bomb was discovered and defused, turning what could have been another catastrophe into a hidden near‑miss in the history of European transport.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: An attack aimed at a symbol of fast, modern mobility is contrasted with an episode about joyful motion and travel, reclaiming trains and roads as spaces of energy rather than fear.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 2006, more than 60 tornadoes tore across the United States in a single day, with Tennessee hit hardest and 29 people killed. The outbreak turned calm spring skies into spiraling columns of wind strong enough to scatter homes like paper.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A violent storm outbreak meets an episode built around weather, rain, and hope, transforming destructive skies into a metaphor for endurance and shared optimism.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 2015, gunmen from the militant group Al‑Shabaab attacked Garissa University College in Kenya, killing at least 148 people and wounding many more. The assault unfolded on a campus—supposedly a place for learning and futures—turning lecture halls into sites of terror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A brutal attack on a university, a place devoted to study, is paired with an episode that gently reclaims learning as joy, resilience, and a long, human process rather than a site of fear.

queue.fact_text: On April 2, 2021, a packed express train in Taiwan slammed into a construction truck that had slid onto the track near Hualien, derailing inside a tunnel and killing at least 49 people. The accident combined bad timing, human error, and unforgiving infrastructure in a narrow mountain pass.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A deadly rail accident shadowing a scenic route contrasts with an episode about safe, improvised overland travel; both live in the world of trains, roads, and the fragile trust we place in journeys.

queue.fact_text: In 1800s Vienna and Leipzig, orchestras played in candlelit halls; by April 2, 1902, Los Angeles had a dedicated movie theater; and by April 2, 1956, television soaps stretched stories across months. Across these April days, humans kept inventing new stages for sound and story.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: This meta‑reflection on evolving stages of listening and watching matches a sonically adventurous episode that travels across genres and eras, like moving between concert halls, cinemas, and living rooms.

queue.fact_text: On different April 2nds, from Ponce de León’s Florida landfall to Zond 1’s Venus‑bound launch, humans pointed themselves toward the unknown—first by ship, then by spacecraft. Each step widened the map, revealing that “here” is always larger than we thought.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A thematic nod to exploration—from oceans to planets—pairs with a "cosmic" dance episode where music itself becomes a vehicle for low‑gravity travel and bright, futuristic motion.

queue.fact_text: Across history, April 2 has seen cities besieged, from Copenhagen’s harbor to the Church of the Nativity, and towns shattered by violence like Bijeljina and Garissa. Yet it has also seen symphonies premiered, cantatas sung, and theaters opened—a reminder that on the same calendar square, humans build both wounds and remedies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: This reflection on a day’s scars and beauty aligns with an episode about accepting imperfections and finding quiet meaning in what doesn’t go to plan.

queue.fact_text: Seen from far away—say, from Sedna’s orbit—April 2 on Earth looks like any other day: clouds moving, lights flickering. Up close, it’s Beethoven in Vienna, Bach in Leipzig, storms over Tennessee, a train in Taiwan, a probe leaving Earth for Venus—24 hours dense with stories on a small, noisy planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A zoomed‑out cosmic view of one Earth day matches an episode about wandering a big city with friends: many stories, one tiny patch of a crowded planet, all stitched together by rhythm.

published.fact_text: In 1800, a 29‑year‑old Ludwig van Beethoven stood before a Viennese audience to conduct the premiere of his Symphony No. 1, a work that gently bent the rules of classical form while still sounding "proper" enough for the time. Hidden in its elegant structure were the first signs of the musical revolutions Beethoven would later unleash.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A boundary‑pushing yet refined symphony matches the episode’s subtle, sophisticated jazz exploration of how form can stretch while staying beautiful.

published.fact_text: On Easter Monday 1725 in Leipzig, Johann Sebastian Bach premiered his cantata "Bleib bei uns, denn es will Abend werden" (BWV 6). Written for the fading light of evening, it weaves voices and instruments into a meditation on dusk, doubt, and the desire for a guiding presence to stay.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An evening‑themed sacred cantata about staying present as night falls resonates with the episode’s slow, contemplative soundtrack for pausing and breathing.

published.fact_text: On April 2, 1513, Juan Ponce de León stepped ashore on the coast of what he named La Florida, likely somewhere between today’s St. Augustine and the mouth of the St. Johns River. His landing stitched European myths of golden lands and fountains of youth onto an already ancient, lived‑in continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A sea voyage into unfamiliar shores pairs with an episode about travel, shifting perspectives, and the soft transformations that happen when you move through new landscapes.

published.fact_text: In 1755, Commodore William James led a daring naval assault that captured the Maratha sea fortress of Suvarnadurg on India’s west coast. The stronghold, perched on rock and surf, had been a symbol of coastal power and was thought nearly impregnable from the sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A naval attack on a coastal fortress fits naturally with a sea‑themed episode recorded from a sailing boat, both living in the world of waves, ships, and salt air.

published.fact_text: On April 2, 1801, the Battle of Copenhagen saw a British fleet smash through a defensive line of Danish and Norwegian ships anchored to protect the harbor. Amid smoke, shattered masts, and a confused chain of command, Admiral Nelson famously ignored an order to withdraw by literally putting his telescope to his blind eye.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The chaos and intensity of a naval battle align with the episode’s high‑voltage rock and blues, a sonic equivalent of cannon blasts and splintering hulls.

published.fact_text: In 1902, the Electric Theatre opened in Los Angeles as the first full‑time movie theater in the United States, charging ten cents for continuous screenings. It turned moving pictures from a passing novelty into a dedicated ritual: a dark room, a beam of light, and strangers gathered to dream together.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: The birth of the dedicated movie theater parallels the launch of a new listening format—both invite people into a darkened, immersive space where sequences of moments become a narrative.

published.fact_text: On April 2, 1912, the RMS Titanic left Belfast Lough for her sea trials, testing engines, steering, and speed in open water. For a brief moment, the “unsinkable” giant performed flawlessly, a gleaming promise of technological mastery just days before her tragic encounter with an iceberg.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A colossal ship on icy seas connects with an Arctic‑water episode; both blend awe at engineering or nature with the cold, cinematic vastness of the ocean.

published.fact_text: On April 2, 1917, U.S. President Woodrow Wilson addressed Congress to ask for a declaration of war against Germany, shifting America from neutrality into the brutal theater of World War I. He framed the conflict as a struggle to make the world “safe for democracy,” while millions braced for trench mud, gas, and loss.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A thunderous political pivot into global war fits with an episode about protest music and confrontation, both steeped in power, anger, and the politics of force.

published.fact_text: On April 2, 1865, the Third Battle of Petersburg shattered Confederate lines, forcing the Army of Northern Virginia and the Confederate government to abandon Richmond. Overnight, the Confederate capital went from symbol of defiance to a city of fires and fleeing officials, foreshadowing the Civil War’s end.
